bibliographic.citation: Hoccleve's Works. III. The Regement of Princes A.D. 1411-12, from the Harleian MS. 4866, and Fourteen of Hoccleve's Minor Poems from the Egerton MS. 615, ed. Frederick J. Furnivall, EETS Extra Series 72 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co., 1897), lxiii + 216 pp.
bibliographic.pages: Target: xxiii-lxii and 1-197; full carrier: lxiii + 216 pp.
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Extra Series 72
bibliographic.title: Hoccleve's Works. III. The Regement of Princes A.D. 1411-12, from the Harleian MS. 4866, and Fourteen of Hoccleve's Minor Poems from the Egerton MS. 615

entities.description: The exact source edition encoded by U-M: fourteen appendix poems on pp. xxiii-lxii and The Regement of Princes on pp. 1-197, with glossary p. lxiii and back matter outside the target boundary.
entities.label: Frederick J. Furnivall, Hoccleve's Works III (1897)
notes: Furnivall's title-page phrase 'fourteen of Hoccleve's minor poems' is a historical edition claim, not a modern resolution of all item-level attributions.
A 1997 Boydell and Brewer reprint is documented by ARLIMA but is not the source of the 2006 U-M OCR.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1897, before the current U.S. public-domain cutoff for published works stated in Copyright Office Circular 15A.
rights.component: The 1897 edition, including its editorial text and published typography

entities.description: The edition-defined selection of thirteen verse passages embedded in the Middle English Pilgrimage of the Soul plus one separate complaint, reordered and printed on pp. xxiii-lxii.
entities.label: Furnivall's fourteen-item Egerton appendix selection
notes: Item 14 occurs earlier in the manuscript but is printed after items 1-13; this editorial order is not manuscript order.

rights.basis: The selected texts are transmitted in a fifteenth-century manuscript; no modern authorship is involved in their underlying medieval wording.
rights.component: The fourteen medieval appendix texts as abstract textual works, distinct from Furnivall's modern-era selection and editing
notes: The entity is the modeled catalog selection because not every individual appendix work has a separate lineage entity; this assertion excludes Furnivall's 1897 selection, order, notes, and typography, which are covered separately.

entities.description: The Middle English prose-and-verse translation in which appendix items 1-13 occur. The translator remains anonymous even where individual embedded lyrics have Hoccleve attributions.
entities.label: The Pilgrimage of the Soul (anonymous Middle English translation)

entities.description: Guillaume de Deguileville's French source work. Source authorship does not identify the author or translator of a Middle English realization.
entities.label: Le Pèlerinage de l'âme

entities.description: Thomas Hoccleve's 5,463-line Middle English mirror for Prince Henry, composed in 1410-1411 and transmitted in at least forty-three surviving manuscripts according to the current METS introduction.
entities.label: The Regement of Princes

rights.basis: The work was composed and transmitted in the fifteenth century; no modern authorship is involved in the medieval text itself.
rights.component: The medieval Regement as an abstract textual work

entities.description: A parchment codex containing the Middle English Pilgrimage of the Soul at fols. 4r-106r. Furnivall selected fourteen discontiguous verse passages; the catalog's French-work title wording is retained as provider description rather than used to settle the English translation's exact source title.
entities.label: British Library, Egerton MS 615
notes: Target loci: item 14 at fols. 8r-13v; items 1-6 at fols. 17v-31v; item 7 at fols. 63r-67v; items 8-13 at fols. 95v-102v.
The current BL record says images are unavailable while also retaining full/selected digital-coverage metadata; no public complete image object was treated as currently obtainable.
Pilgrimage contents at fols. 4r-106r; table of contents at fols. 1r-3v.
Catalog origin: eastern England, perhaps Norfolk or Lincolnshire.

entities.description: Furnivall's base manuscript for The Regement of Princes, selected for its Chaucer portrait, final-e practice, and older readings. Its first leaf is a later replacement; the medieval manuscript lacks the presentation leaf and final leaf.
entities.label: British Library, Harley MS 4866
notes: Furnivall's base-text use must not be generalized to the Royal-supplied presentation block or envoy.
Produced in London or Westminster.
The first page is an early-modern replacement; the last five lines are reported in the same later hand by the current catalog.

entities.description: A mid-fifteenth-century parchment manuscript containing The Regement of Princes at fols. 4r-101v. Wright based his 1860 edition on it; Furnivall collated it throughout and used it directly for missing Harley passages.
entities.label: British Library, Royal MS 17 D VI

notes: The Regement occupies fols. 4r-101v.

entities.description: A 49-leaf parchment miscellany written in anglicana by Thomas Hoccleve. It contains an incomplete holograph Complaint of the Virgin at fols. 3r-7v and the Regement envoy at fols. 39v-40r among nineteen poems.
entities.label: Huntington Library, MS HM 111
notes: The Complaint copy begins at Furnivall's stanza 7; it cannot supply the first six stanzas of the complete Egerton text.
Written in anglicana by Hoccleve; nineteen poems; Middle English with Middle French rubrics.

next_steps: Follow the bibliography and manuscript links in each of the nine DIMEV records currently assigning Hoccleve.
Retain each result as an item-level attribution unless independent evidence supports a stronger status.
Do not use the anonymous carrier translator or Guillaume's French-source authorship as a substitute for English lyric responsibility.
openQuestions.question: Which of the thirteen English Pilgrimage lyrics besides the Complaint of the Virgin can be assigned to Hoccleve, and what item-specific textual or manuscript evidence supports each assignment?

next_steps: Inspect the four records' cited scholarship and witnesses individually.
Preserve Guillaume as author of the relevant French source passages unless a source explicitly identifies an English author or translator.
Keep appendix item 14 separate from the thirteen Pilgrimage-embedded lyrics and preserve Furnivall's reordered printed sequence.
openQuestions.question: What exact source and responsibility relationships underlie DIMEV records 2860, 443, 407, and 878, especially the separately transmitted Pietous complainte of the Soule?

next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Collections for item-specific production metadata and the status of the historical TIFF masters.
Request a stable complete image inventory or manifest if one exists.
Do not infer complete physical-carrier coverage from the PB count or from the narrower encoded intellectual text.
openQuestions.question: Which physical copy and production workflow generated U-M's 292 historical page-image filenames, and do those images represent every leaf of the scanned 1897 carrier?

next_steps: Ask U-M to clarify which terms currently govern reuse of the XML, OCR, and page images.
Continue to record NoC-US as a territorial rights statement rather than a license.
Keep the legacy provider terms visible until the provider supplies a superseding item-specific statement.
openQuestions.question: How should U-M's current public-domain/NoC-US statements be reconciled with the where-applicable copyright and distribution-restriction language embedded in the 2006 XML?

next_steps: Recheck each catalog record after British Library digital-service restoration milestones.
Request one item-specific quotation covering required folios, delivery resolution, reuse terms, and any condition restrictions before budgeting.
Treat the cached generic rate card as supporting service evidence only.
openQuestions.question: Will complete public images for Egerton MS 615, Harley MS 4866, and Royal MS 17 D VI return, or can complete research reproductions currently be ordered?

next_steps: Compare Furnivall's opening apparatus line by line against current images or an authorized reproduction of Harley's replacement and Royal's opening.
Keep the later replacement, Royal-supplied medieval text, and ordinary Royal collation as separate layers.
openQuestions.question: How should each of the first 56 Regement lines in Furnivall's printed text be apportioned between the early-modern Harley replacement and Royal-derived correction or supplementation?

reviewNotes: The cataloging subject is the exact electronic/edition composite, not all three volumes named by the U-M header, not a complete Hoccleve canon, and not one medieval work.

Appendix items 1-13 are verse passages embedded in the anonymous Middle English Pilgrimage of the Soul; item 14 is a separate Egerton passage printed last by Furnivall. Guillaume de Deguileville's French-source authorship is never substituted for authorship or translation of the Middle English realizations.

DIMEV's current item-level records assign ten appendix poems to Hoccleve, including the securely supported Complaint of the Virgin. The other nine are retained as catalog attributions pending item-specific source review; the Middle English Pilgrimage translator remains anonymous.

Harley 4866 is the Regement base; Royal 17 D VI is both a continuous collate and the direct source for specified missing passages. These roles are separate relations rather than a blended two-manuscript source label.

HM 111 contains two discontiguous target mappings: the incomplete Complaint of the Virgin at fols. 3r-7v and the Regement envoy at fols. 39v-40r. Complete-codex coverage does not imply that intervening folios contain the target.

The 292 historical U-M TIFF page images named in the XML are modeled separately from the current public U-M text/page-view access layer; no item-specific production record or complete direct-download inventory was found.

Current U-M No Copyright-United States labeling conflicts with the legacy 'where applicable' and distribution-restriction text embedded in the 2006 XML. Both provider assertions are retained as an unresolved terms question.

British Library catalog records currently expose conflicting signals: digitized/full-coverage metadata survives, but target manuscript images are marked unavailable after the cyber-attack. Public catalog access, onsite manuscript access, and paid/requested reproduction are therefore separate records.

Every cached local copy states target_work_presence. Supporting metadata, scan-data, legal evidence, the target-absent 1925 volume II, and the BL rate card are marked absent without a fabricated work_portion.

Public-domain conclusions are jurisdiction- and component-specific. Provider image statements, service terms, modern transcription licenses, and repository output licensing remain distinct from the medieval text and nineteenth-century editions.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is the pinned General Corpus copy of University of Michigan CME item ADQ4048.0001.001, a 2006 uncorrected OCR/TEI-in-Libraries-Level-1 encoding of Frederick J. Furnivall's 1897 EETS Extra Series 72 volume III. Its exact target is composite: fourteen appendix poems on pp. xxiii-lxii and the complete Regement of Princes on pp. 1-197; p. lxiii, other front matter, pp. 199-216, advertisements, and volumes I-II are omitted. Furnivall's edition transcribes and prints the appendix's fourteen discontiguous passages from Egerton MS 615, keeping thirteen embedded Pilgrimage of the Soul lyrics distinct from a separate complaint; the preface credits Henry Littlehales collectively with copying most of this material without identifying exact items. The Regement base was Harley MS 4866, continuously collated with Royal MS 17 D VI; Royal directly supplies the missing five-stanza presentation block and final envoy. Huntington HM 111 is a separate Hoccleve holograph parallel for the incomplete Complaint of the Virgin and the Regement envoy. Complete BSB volume-I and volume-III carriers, the complete HM 111 codex, Wright's 1860 Royal-based edition, pinned Hoccleve Archive transcriptions, and a complete Google Books page-image sequence for target-absent volume II are cached with exact checksums and target-presence mappings.

catalogSummary.author: Thomas Hoccleve as author of The Regement of Princes; supported as translator of appendix item 7; attributed author in current DIMEV records for nine further appendix lyrics; responsibility for the remaining four Middle English realizations is unresolved
catalogSummary.date: The Regement composed 1410-1411; Middle English Pilgrimage carrier traditionally dated 1413; exact edited composite published 1897
catalogSummary.title: Hoccleve's Works III: The Regement of Princes and Furnivall's fourteen-item appendix selection

catalogingSubject.label: Hoccleve's Works III: The Regement of Princes and Furnivall's fourteen-item appendix selection
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The exact intellectual/content unit delimited by the pinned U-M XML and Frederick J. Furnivall's 1897 EETS volume III: (1) fourteen separately headed verse items printed in the appendix on pp. xxiii-lxii, of which the first thirteen occur within the anonymous Middle English Pilgrimage of the Soul and the fourteenth is a separate complaint in Egerton MS 615; and (2) the complete printed Regement of Princes on pp. 1-197. It excludes the glossary on p. lxiii, all other front matter, the glossary and index on pp. 199-216, advertisements, and the broader three-volume Hoccleve series named in the electronic header. The cataloging unit is neither one medieval work nor a complete Hoccleve canon.

contentParts.label: Fourteen-item appendix selection from Egerton MS 615
notes: Furnivall prints manuscript item 14 last although it precedes the other selected passages in Egerton MS 615.
contentParts.summary: Furnivall's appendix prints thirteen lyrics embedded in the Middle English Pilgrimage of the Soul followed by a separate complaint; manuscript order is not preserved and the first thirteen/items 1-13 must not be conflated with item 14.

contentParts.label: Carrier context: thirteen verse passages in The Pilgrimage of the Soul

locators.value: discontiguous embedded lyrics at fols. 17v-31v, 63r-67v, and 95v-102v

notes: Item-level authorship and translation responsibilities remain attached only where separately evidenced.
contentParts.summary: A zero-count structural grouping for appendix items 1-13 as passages embedded in the anonymous Middle English Pilgrimage carrier. It records carrier and translation context without assigning the carrier translator to each lyric.

contentParts.label: The epistle of grace sent to the seek man. littera.
contentParts.summary: A 26-stanza, 182-line rhyme-royal letter of spiritual counsel spoken by Grace, printed as the first Pilgrimage lyric in the appendix.

contentParts.label: The chartre of pardon
contentParts.summary: A 14-stanza, 98-line rhyme-royal charter of pardon within the Middle English Pilgrimage, with English responsibility unresolved beyond the anonymous carrier translation.

contentParts.label: Cantus peregrinorum
contentParts.summary: A five-stanza, 35-line Pilgrimage song assigned to Hoccleve by DIMEV record 2065.

contentParts.label: Cantus angelorum
contentParts.summary: A five-stanza, 35-line angels' song within the Middle English Pilgrimage; DIMEV links Guillaume as source author without thereby identifying the English versifier.

contentParts.label: The aungelys song within
contentParts.summary: A seven-stanza, 49-line angels' song within the Middle English Pilgrimage; French-source authorship and English realization remain distinct.

contentParts.label: The Angels' Song. Honour to Jesus.
contentParts.summary: A five-stanza, 35-line devotional song assigned to Hoccleve by DIMEV record 2064.

contentParts.label: A lamentacioun of the grene tree, complaynyng of the losyng of hire appill

locators.value: 3r-7v; incomplete parallel lacking the first six stanzas

contentParts.summary: A forty-stanza, 280-line Marian lament, now commonly treated as Hoccleve's Complaint of the Virgin; HM 111 preserves a holograph parallel beginning with stanza 7.

contentParts.label: The recordyng of aungeles song of the Natiuite of oure lady
contentParts.summary: A seven-stanza, 49-line song for the Nativity of the Virgin assigned to Hoccleve by DIMEV record 2063.

contentParts.label: The aungeles song
contentParts.summary: A two-stanza, 14-line angels' song assigned to Hoccleve by DIMEV record 2061.

contentParts.label: The aungeles songe in the feste of the Epiphanie of oure lord
contentParts.summary: A four-stanza, 28-line Epiphany song assigned to Hoccleve by DIMEV record 2060.

contentParts.label: The aungeles song on pask day
contentParts.summary: A five-stanza, 35-line Easter song assigned to Hoccleve by DIMEV record 2067.

contentParts.label: The song of graces of alle seintes upon Paske day
contentParts.summary: A four-stanza, 28-line Easter song of the saints assigned to Hoccleve by DIMEV record 2062.

contentParts.label: The aungeles song & alle othir seintes in the feste of Pentecost
contentParts.summary: A three-stanza, 21-line Pentecost song assigned to Hoccleve by DIMEV record 2066.

contentParts.label: The pietous complainte of the Soule
contentParts.summary: A 52-stanza, 364-line complaint copied from an earlier, separate Egerton passage and printed last by Furnivall; it is not one of the thirteen embedded Pilgrimage lyrics grouped by his appendix heading.

contentParts.label: The Regement of Princes
contentParts.summary: Hoccleve's 5,463-line rhyme-royal mirror for Prince Henry combines an autobiographical opening and dialogue about poverty and patronage with a formal address, fifteen sections of princely counsel, and an envoy to the book.

contentParts.label: Opening meditation
contentParts.summary: Hoccleve reflects on worldly instability, poverty, thought, and writing before the encounter with the old man.

contentParts.label: Meeting and dialogue with an old man
notes: The XML repeats Furnivall's heading 'old beggar'; the current METS edition describes the interlocutor as an old man, so the normalized label avoids making beggar status a settled identity.
contentParts.summary: An old man questions and counsels Hoccleve about poverty, annuity, service, writing, and seeking princely patronage.

contentParts.label: Address and proem to Prince Henry
notes: The first five stanzas, lines 2017-2051, are supplied in Furnivall from Royal MS 17 D VI because Harley 4866 lacks the presentation leaf.
contentParts.summary: Hoccleve addresses the prince, presents his book, invokes Chaucer and named authorities, and turns from the dialogue toward formal counsel.

contentParts.label: Fifteen sections of princely counsel
contentParts.summary: The main advice sequence treats royal dignity and oaths, justice, law, pity, mercy, patience, chastity, magnanimity, riches, largesse, avarice, prudence, counsel, and peace.

contentParts.label: On the dignity of a king
contentParts.summary: The first counsel section defines royal dignity and the obligations attached to it.

contentParts.label: On coronation oaths, truth, and cautious speech
contentParts.summary: Counsel on a king's oaths, truthfulness, promises, and disciplined speech.

contentParts.label: Of justice
contentParts.summary: Counsel on impartial justice and the harms caused by its corruption.

contentParts.label: On observing the laws
contentParts.summary: Counsel that rulers honor and uphold the laws by which their people are governed.

contentParts.label: Of pity
contentParts.summary: Counsel on pity as compassionate assistance and a royal virtue.

contentParts.label: Of mercy
contentParts.summary: Counsel on mercy, forgiveness, and relief of another's suffering.

contentParts.label: Of patience
contentParts.summary: Counsel on patience, endurance, charity, and mastery of anger.

contentParts.label: Of chastity
contentParts.summary: Counsel on chastity and resistance to sexual vice.

contentParts.label: Of royal magnanimity
contentParts.summary: Counsel on magnanimity, honor, and the prince's proper estimation of worldly rank.

contentParts.label: That a king should not place happiness in riches
contentParts.summary: Counsel against treating wealth as the source of royal happiness.

contentParts.label: Of largesse and prodigality
notes: A nested editorial note contains a three-line quotation encoded as LG/L; those note elements are excluded from this part's metrics.
contentParts.summary: Counsel distinguishing virtuous largesse from wasteful prodigality.

contentParts.label: Of avarice
contentParts.summary: Counsel against avarice and insatiable accumulation.

contentParts.label: Of royal prudence
contentParts.summary: Counsel on prudence, foresight, and discerning governance.

contentParts.label: On taking counsel in all actions
contentParts.summary: Counsel on selecting advisers and seeking deliberation before action.

contentParts.label: Of peace
contentParts.summary: Counsel on internal and international peace, Christian concord, war, and the duties of rulers.

contentParts.label: The envoy
notes: Furnivall supplies the edition's envoy from Royal MS 17 D VI because Harley 4866 has lost its last leaf; HM 111 is a separate holograph parallel, not the printed base for the edition's supplied passage.
contentParts.summary: Three eight-line stanzas address the book as it approaches Prince Henry and ask that its plain style and faults be excused.

notes: Furnivall's title says A.D. 1411-12; the current METS introduction dates composition to 1410-11. The difference is preserved rather than silently harmonized.

dateStatements.display: Traditionally 1413 for the Middle English Pilgrimage of the Soul

scope.description: Date of the English Pilgrimage carrier tradition, not an individual composition or translation date for any embedded lyric
Publication of the exact two-part edited composite cataloged here

extent.description: Fourteen appendix poems (179 direct LG, 1,253 L) plus the complete Regement (780 direct LG, 5,463 L). The 37 modeled content parts comprise the appendix container, a zero-count Pilgrimage-carrier grouping, fourteen poem children, the Regement parent, five major Regement divisions, and fifteen nested advice sections. Raw XML descendant counts are 960 LG and 6,719 L because an editorial note inside the largesse section embeds one LG and three quoted L elements; those are excluded from intellectual-text totals.

assertion.notes: Raw descendant counts are 6,719 L and 960 LG because one Regement editorial note contains a nested three-line verse quotation and nested LG; those note elements are excluded from intellectual-text metrics.

formStatements.description: Fifteen verse works totaling 6,716 intellectual verse lines in 959 direct stanza/verse-group elements.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Latin rubrics, marginal source quotations, and brief French manuscript headings belong to embedded or transmission layers and do not displace Middle English as the primary language of the selected verse.
Content-part titles retain Furnivall/XML wording where it identifies exact items; interpretive normalization is confined to catalog labels and summaries.
The title-page phrase 'fourteen of Hoccleve's minor poems' is historical edition evidence. It is not used to erase item-level authorship uncertainty.
Guillaume de Deguileville is modeled as author of a French source work, never automatically as author or translator of a Middle English realization.
The Regement's editorially supplied and collated witness relationships belong to lineage; work metadata records their consequence only where it affects exact content boundaries.
Furnivall's p. lxiii glossary and all material after the Regement on p. 197 are outside the cataloging subject even though they remain in complete cached carriers.

next_steps: Review DIMEV's cited bibliography and current specialist studies item by item.
Keep the anonymous carrier translator, DIMEV catalog attributions, and Guillaume's French-source authorship as distinct assertions.
openQuestions.question: Which of the thirteen English Pilgrimage lyrics besides the Complaint of the Virgin can be assigned to Hoccleve, and on what poem-specific evidence?

next_steps: Inspect current scholarship and DIMEV record 878's sources without treating Guillaume's source field as an English authorship claim.
Preserve the separate Egerton locus and Furnivall's rearranged printed order.
openQuestions.question: What is the precise source, authorship, and relationship of The pietous complainte of the Soule to the Middle English Pilgrimage carrier?

next_steps: Request current British Library images or onsite consultation of Harley 4866 and Royal 17 D VI.
Retain Furnivall's explicit supplied blocks and continuous H/R apparatus meanwhile.
openQuestions.question: Can direct manuscript inspection refine which exact Regement readings come from Harley's medieval hand, its replacement leaves, Royal 17 D VI, and Furnivall's editorial intervention?

next_steps: Seek item-level U-M production records and scan provenance.
Do not infer the scanned copy from the BSB or Google carriers.

summaries.value: This electronic/edition composite joins fourteen religious verse items from Egerton MS 615 to Thomas Hoccleve's Regement of Princes. The first thirteen appendix items are embedded lyrics from the anonymous Middle English Pilgrimage of the Soul; item 14 is a separate complaint, and item 7 has an incomplete Hoccleve holograph parallel. The Regement moves from an opening meditation and long dialogue with an old man into an address to Prince Henry and fifteen sections of political, ethical, and religious counsel, ending with a three-stanza envoy.

Included: appendix pp. xxiii-lxii and Regement pp. 1-197. Excluded: all other front matter, p. lxiii glossary, p. 198 boundary matter, glossary/index pp. 199-216, advertisements, volumes I-II, and the revised 1970 reprint. Historical titles and attributions remain edition-layer evidence rather than automatic whole-unit metadata.

notes: The wording keeps the two-part electronic/edition boundary visible and does not imply uniform authorship across the appendix.

work.author: Thomas Hoccleve as author of The Regement of Princes; supported as translator of appendix item 7; attributed author in current DIMEV records for nine further appendix lyrics; responsibility for the remaining four Middle English realizations is unresolved

work.summary: This electronic/edition composite joins fourteen religious verse items from Egerton MS 615 to Thomas Hoccleve's Regement of Princes. The first thirteen appendix items are embedded lyrics from the anonymous Middle English Pilgrimage of the Soul; item 14 is a separate complaint, and item 7 has an incomplete Hoccleve holograph parallel. The Regement moves from an opening meditation and long dialogue with an old man into an address to Prince Henry and fifteen sections of political, ethical, and religious counsel, ending with a three-stanza envoy.
